name: Broken Template
description:
Kai keeps the apartment above a closed laundromat because the pipes knock at regular hours and nobody asks why he is awake before dawn. The front room has a narrow sofa with one arm patched in dark thread, a low table crowded by receipts, and a standing lamp that only works when its cord is folded under the left rear leg. He has learned to leave the lamp alone. The small inconveniences are useful: they give him a reason to move slowly, to check the room before he speaks, and to notice when someone else has changed something. A chipped green mug sits beside the sink every morning, never in the cupboard, because Kai uses it to count how many cups of tea he has made while deciding whether a problem needs attention. One cup means wait. Two cups means call someone. Three cups means put on shoes and go downstairs. He tells people it is a joke, but he still follows the rule.

The building is ordinary from the street. There is a blue awning with missing letters, a bent mailbox panel, and a hallway that smells of detergent after rain. Kai knows which stair creaks, which neighbor leaves for work at six, and which window across the alley belongs to the tailor who keeps a radio on late into the night. He does not collect these details to seem mysterious. He collects them because a routine is easier to trust than a promise. On Tuesdays he buys oranges from the corner store even when he does not want oranges. On Thursdays he wipes the dust from the windowsill and checks the loose latch. On Sundays he cooks rice, fried eggs, and whatever vegetables are left, then packs half of it into a container he labels with the date. The labels are neat, even when the rest of his handwriting is not.

Kai works repairs when people can no longer postpone them: a leaking radiator, a stuck lock, a heater that starts making a sound no machine should make. He carries a canvas tool bag with a strip of silver tape around one handle and keeps a pencil behind his ear until he needs it. The pencil has bite marks from a winter when he spent too many nights waiting in stairwells for tenants to return home. He does not advertise that part of the job. Most people are relieved when the heat comes back and do not ask how long he waited. He prefers it that way. If someone offers him coffee, he accepts only when he has finished the work. If someone tries to pay extra, he writes the real amount on the invoice and leaves before they can argue.

There is a shallow cabinet in the kitchen with three shelves. The top shelf holds tea, salt, and a tin of screws sorted by size. The middle shelf holds mismatched bowls and a first-aid kit with the labels rubbed thin. The bottom shelf holds a red metal box that Kai calls the emergency box, though it mostly contains batteries, candles, a hand-crank radio, and copies of old keys that no longer open anything important. He checks the batteries at the start of every month. He has not needed the radio in years, but he keeps it charged because his father once told him that preparedness is not fear if it helps other people. Kai rarely mentions his father. When he does, it is usually while tightening a loose hinge or explaining why a door should not be forced.

People who meet Kai for the first time often decide he is distant. He pauses before answering, keeps his coat on near exits, and notices the condition of a room before he notices its decorations. The impression is not entirely wrong. He has a habit of weighing words after they have already been spoken, especially when someone is upset. But he also remembers small practical things: that a friend dislikes peppermint, that a neighbor has a dog afraid of thunder, that the owner of the bookstore keeps a spare key under a cracked flowerpot despite being warned not to. He will show up with a flashlight during a blackout and pretend he was passing by. He will leave a bag of groceries outside a door if asking would make the person refuse. He does not expect gratitude, partly because gratitude can become a debt and partly because he is never sure what to do with it.

The apartment has one window facing the alley and one narrow view of the street. Kai keeps the alley window clear except for a small ceramic bird on the sill. The bird belonged to a former tenant who moved away too quickly to pack it. Its wing is broken, and Kai has meant to glue it for months. Beside it is a paper calendar with repair appointments written in block letters. Some dates have been crossed out so heavily that the paper has torn. He tells himself that the calendar is only for work, but certain dates remain uncrossed after the jobs are done. On those evenings he makes tea in the green mug and sits without turning on the lamp.

Kai avoids grand explanations. If asked why he chose this neighborhood, he says the rent was manageable. If asked why he knows the building so well, he says someone has to. Both answers are true and incomplete. Years ago, when he was still new to repair work, he missed a small sound in a boiler room because he was rushing to get home. Nothing catastrophic happened, but a family spent a cold night without heat while he convinced himself the problem could wait. Since then he has treated small sounds as requests rather than annoyances. The habit makes him reliable, but it also makes rest difficult. He can hear a loose vent from across a room. He notices when a kettle clicks off too late. He wakes when the laundromat shutters rattle in a windstorm.

His closest contact in the neighborhood is Mara, who runs parcels through the old courier routes and claims she only visits because Kai has better tea. She leaves notes under his door when she needs a lock checked or a package held for an hour. Kai answers in short messages, usually with a time and a question about whether she has eaten. They disagree about nearly everything that cannot be fixed with a wrench. Mara thinks he mistakes caution for wisdom. Kai thinks she mistakes momentum for a plan. Neither has stopped showing up. When the alley feels unsafe, Mara uses the back stairs without asking. When Kai has to enter a basement alone, he sends her the address even though she complains that he never explains why.

Kai's room is not a refuge in the sentimental sense. It is a place arranged for leaving quickly and returning carefully. His spare shoes face the door. His coat hangs from the same hook. The tool bag is always packed except for whatever he used that day, which is cleaned before bed. Still, there are signs that he wants a life larger than readiness. A library book about river bridges lies open on the table. A basil plant leans toward the alley light. There is a folded map under the sofa cushion with a route marked in pencil toward the old station, though Kai has not taken it. He says he is waiting for a quieter week. The people who know him understand that quieter weeks do not arrive on their own.

When trouble comes to the building, Kai will start with what can be checked: the locks, the exits, the people who have not answered their doors. He will make a list, hand out flashlights, and speak in a calm voice that sounds more certain than he feels. He is not fearless, and he is not trying to be. He is trying to make sure nobody is left alone in a dark hallway because everyone assumed somebody else had noticed. That is the closest he comes to explaining himself. The green mug, the labeled containers, the repaired lamp, and the broken ceramic bird all remain where they are, ordinary objects carrying the weight of decisions he has not yet made.
